reasons: Fungal infection caused by Venturia inaequalis
Cool and wet weather conditions during spring
Poor air circulation around the tree canopy
Infected fallen leaves left on the ground from previous season
High humidity promoting spore germination and spread
solutions: Apply fungicides (captan or myclobutanil) during early spring
Rake and destroy fallen infected leaves to reduce spore source
Prune trees to improve air circulation and reduce humidity
Plant scab-resistant apple varieties in future plantings
Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip irrigation to keep foliage dry

reasons: Fungal infection caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa
Warm and humid weather conditions
Wounds or injuries on the tree from insects or pruning
Infected mummified fruits left on the tree or ground
Weakened trees due to poor nutrition or stress
solutions: Remove and destroy infected fruits, leaves, and dead wood
Apply copper-based or thiophanate-methyl fungicides
Prune out cankers and dead branches during dry weather
Maintain proper tree nutrition with balanced fertilization
Control insects that create entry wounds for the fungus

reasons: Fungal infection caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae
Presence of nearby cedar or juniper trees acting as alternate hosts
Wet and mild spring conditions enabling spore release
Wind carrying spores from infected cedar/juniper to apple trees
Susceptible apple varieties planted near cedar trees
solutions: Remove nearby cedar or juniper trees that serve as alternate hosts
Apply fungicides (myclobutanil or mancozeb) during spring
Plant rust-resistant apple varieties
Monitor and remove orange gelatinous galls from nearby cedars
Apply protective sprays before and during wet spring weather

reasons: No disease detected

solutions: Continue regular monitoring for early signs of disease

reasons: Fungal infection caused by Podosphaera clandestina
Warm days and cool nights creating ideal conditions
High humidity without actual rainfall
Dense canopy reducing air flow around leaves
Excessive nitrogen fertilization promoting lush, susceptible growth
solutions: Apply sulfur-based or potassium bicarbonate fungicides
Prune trees to open up the canopy and improve air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Remove and destroy infected plant parts promptly
Use resistant cherry varieties in future plantings

reasons: No disease detected

solutions: Continue regular pruning to maintain open canopy

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.disease: Cercospora Leaf Spot / Gray Leaf Spot
reasons: Fungal infection caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis
Extended periods of high humidity and warm temperatures
Continuous corn cropping without rotation
Infected crop residue left in the field
Reduced air movement in dense plantings
solutions: Rotate crops with non-host plants like soybean or wheat
Plow or till under infected crop residue after harvest
Plant resistant corn hybrids
Apply fungicides (strobilurins or triazoles) at early disease onset
Avoid overly dense planting to improve air circulation

reasons: Fungal infection caused by Puccinia sorghi
Cool temperatures (60–77°F) combined with high humidity
Wind-driven spores spreading from infected plants
Planting susceptible corn varieties
Late planting exposing plants during high-risk weather periods
solutions: Plant rust-resistant or tolerant corn hybrids
Apply fungicides (triazoles or strobilurins) when rust first appears
Plant early to avoid peak rust infection periods
Monitor fields regularly for early pustule development
Avoid overhead irrigation that keeps foliage wet

reasons: Fungal infection caused by Exserohilum turcicum
Moderate temperatures (65–80°F) with prolonged leaf wetness
Infected crop debris left in the field
Planting highly susceptible corn hybrids
Continuous corn monoculture without rotation
solutions: Use resistant corn hybrids with NLB tolerance genes
Rotate with non-host crops to reduce soilborne inoculum
Apply foliar fungicides at or before tasseling
Manage crop residue by tillage after harvest
Plant at proper density to allow good air movement

reasons: No disease detected
Good field management and crop rotation in practice
Appropriate hybrid selection for local conditions
Proper fertilization and irrigation
Timely planting reducing disease exposure
solutions: Continue crop rotation with legumes or small grains
Scout fields regularly through the season
Maintain balanced nitrogen to avoid excessive lush growth
Ensure good soil drainage to prevent stress
Keep records of hybrids and disease history for future planning

reasons: Fungal infection caused by Guignardia bidwellii
Warm and wet conditions during early growing season
Infected mummified berries or canes left in the vineyard
Poor canopy management leading to dense foliage
Delayed or missed fungicide applications early in season
solutions: Apply fungicides (myclobutanil or mancozeb) from bud break onward
Remove and destroy mummified berries and infected canes
Prune vines to improve canopy airflow and light penetration
Practice thorough vineyard sanitation after harvest
Use a consistent spray program during wet springs

reasons: Complex fungal disease involving Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and others
Infection entering through pruning wounds
Stress factors like drought or extreme temperatures worsening symptoms
Old vines more susceptible due to accumulated wood infections
Waterlogged soils weakening vine root systems
solutions: Apply wound protectants immediately after pruning
Prune during dry weather to reduce fungal spore dispersal
Remove and burn heavily infected vines
Avoid large pruning cuts; use double pruning techniques
Maintain vine health through proper irrigation and nutrition

reasons: Fungal infection caused by Pseudocercospora vitis
High humidity and prolonged wet weather
Poor air circulation in the vineyard canopy
Infected plant debris serving as overwintering inoculum
Late season infections when plants are under stress
solutions: Apply copper-based or mancozeb fungicides preventively
Prune and train vines to open up the canopy
Remove infected leaves and plant debris
Ensure good drainage to reduce humidity at plant level
Scout regularly and apply fungicides at first sign of disease

reasons: No disease detected

solutions: Continue preventive fungicide sprays during wet periods

Monitor for pests like grape berry moth and leafhoppers
Test soil regularly and adjust nutrition as needed
Keep vineyard floor managed to reduce disease inoculum

Spread by Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri) insect vector

solutions: Control Asian citrus psyllid with insecticides (imidacloprid, etc.)
Remove and destroy infected trees to prevent further spread
Use certified disease-free planting material only
Apply nutritional sprays to manage symptoms in mildly affected trees
Implement area-wide psyllid management programs in coordination with neighbors

solutions: Apply copper-based bactericides during the dormant season
Plant resistant or tolerant peach varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness

Apply protective copper sprays before and during rainy periods

Remove and destroy infected plant material from the field

reasons: Infestation by Tetranychus urticae mites
